context: whom I would have retained with me that in thy stead he might have ministered unto me in the bonds of the gospel
point_summary: Paul's willingness to send Onesimus back rather than retain him for gospel ministry illustrates that he refused to act unilaterally without Philemon's consent.

context: but without my mind what I do nothing
point_summary: Paul's refusal to retain Onesimus without Philemon's consent illustrates that genuine Christian benefit must be freely given, not compelled.

context: but how much more unto thee
point_summary: Onesimus's conversion elevates his status from runaway slave to beloved brother, with Paul pressing Philemon that this new spiritual bond obligates an even deeper reception than Paul himself feels.

context: Paul talks about not only hearing of his love but also his faith in verse number five of the book of Philemon
point_summary: Philemon's established love and faith, noted in verse 5, mark him as the spiritually mature believer whom Paul therefore holds responsible to lead in forgiveness and reconciliation toward Onesimus.

context: Paul says to Philemon, receive him, receive Onesimus as myself
point_summary: Receiving a wrongdoer as one would receive the apostle himself is "graduate-level Christianity" that surpasses fleshly capacity and requires Spirit-enabled forgiveness modeled on God's own character.

context: ...a man that's mentioned just three times Colossians 1 Colossians 4 and Philemon...
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I beseech thee for my son Onesimus
point_summary: Paul's calling Onesimus "my son" illustrates that spiritual new birth—being begotten in Christ—creates a genuine father-son relationship in the faith, even when that gospel witness happens from a prison cell.

context: But in verse number five, the book of Philemon describes Philemon as a man that loved the Lord and loved all the saints.
point_summary: Philemon's commendation for loving "all saints"—not merely those he connected with best—illustrates that genuine love for God extends without partiality to every believer.

context: but God is really, in this book of Philemon, he's setting Philemon up for Onesimus.
point_summary: Philemon's commendation for loving "all saints"—not merely those he connected with best—is used to illustrate that genuine love for God extends without partiality to every believer, including the formerly estranged Onesimus.

context: hearing of thy love and faith, which thou hast toward the Lord Jesus and toward all saints
point_summary: Philemon's example illustrates the inseparable pairing of love for Christ and love for fellow believers as twin marks of genuine Christian obedience.

context: turn to the book of Philemon, and be looking at just a verse of Scripture here from the book of Philemon. There's only one chapter, so turn to chapter number one
point_summary: Paul's wide-ranging missionary journeys produced lasting personal relationships, many of which are preserved by name in his epistles, including the opening of Philemon.

context: Paul, a prisoner of Jesus Christ, and Timothy, our brother, unto Philemon, our dearly beloved and fellow laborer.
point_summary: Paul's self-identification as "a prisoner of Jesus Christ" in Philemon's opening verse establishes the letter as one of the prison epistles written during his first Roman confinement, situating it alongside Colossians in time and origin.

context: And to our beloved Athia and Archippus, our fellow soldier, and to the church in thy house.
point_summary: Philemon 1:2 is noted as establishing the letter's recipients—Apphia, Archippus, and the house church—and its historical context as one of Paul's prison epistles written during his first Roman confinement.

context: We notice in the first verse of Philemon, that as he speaks about Philemon, he uses this terminology, our dearly beloved and fellow laborer.
point_summary: In Philemon 1:1, Paul calls Philemon a "fellow laborer" rather than "fellow soldier," a distinction that sets up the sermon's focus on Archippus as one of only two individuals Paul specifically designates with the rarer, more militant title.

context: ...the only two verses of Scripture that we have specifically about this man archivist or the two that we've looked at this evening Philemon one verse number two...
point_summary: Philemon 1:2 is one of only two biblical references to Archippus, leaving his ministry circumstances—whether he quit or merely wavered—historically uncertain but spiritually instructive.

context: but in Philemon in verse number 10, it says, I beseech thee for my son Onesimus, whom I have begotten in my bonds.
point_summary: Onesimus's conversion, described in Philemon 10 as Paul begetting him "in my bonds," illustrates that Paul's imprisonment became an instrument of gospel fruitfulness rather than a hindrance to ministry.

context: We read of this person, Archippus, we read of him in Philemon too and it appears from that portion of Scripture in verse number two of the book of Philemon
point_summary: Philemon 1:2 is cited to identify Archippus as likely a pastor who had grown discouraged and possibly abandoned his ministry, providing context for the urgent charge in Colossians 4:17.

context: hearing of thy love and faith which thou hast toward the Lord Jesus and toward all saints
point_summary: Philemon's commendation for loving both the Lord Jesus and all the saints illustrates the dual object of Christian love: the Redeemer and the redeemed.

context: In Philemon, there's only one chapter, verse number 4, I thank my God making mention of thee always in my prayers.
point_summary: Philemon 1:4 is cited alongside other Pauline letters to establish a consistent pattern: Paul habitually named specific individuals before God, modeling intercessory prayer as a regular, personal practice.

context: I thank my God making mention of thee always in my prayers
point_summary: Philemon 1:4 is cited as evidence that Paul maintained consistent, habitual prayer for others—not merely teaching prayer but personally practicing it without ceasing.
